currentContent: > 2.1. Overview. In the high-dimensional factor model, let $\Gamma$ be the p × K matrix of loadings. Then the general model (1.1) can be written as (2.1) $Y = \Gamma F' + U$. Suppose we additionally observe a set of covariates $\{X_i\}_{i≤p}$. The basic idea of the Projected-PCA is to {{c1 smooth the observations $\{Y_{it}\}_{i≤p}$for each given day t against its associated covariates}} . More speciﬁcally, let $\{\hat{Y}_{it}\}_{i≤p}$ be the ﬁtted value after regressing {{c1 $\{Y_{it}\}_{i≤p}$ on$\{X_{i}\}_{i≤p}$ for each given t}}. This results in {{c1 a smooth or projected observation matrix $\hat{Y}$}}, which will also be denoted by $PY$. The Projected-PCA then estimates the factors and loadings by {{c1 running the PCA based on the projected data $\hat{Y}$}}. currentContent:
Consider following package structure
```
project
├── __init__.py
└── src
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── a
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   └── b.py
    └── c
        ├── __init__.py
        └── d.py
```
with following code for `b.py` and `d.py`
```python
# b.py
from ..c import d
print(__package__)
print(__name__)


## d.py
print(__package__)
print(__name__)

```
in directory a/
run `python b.py` will {{c1 not work:: will relative import work or not}} as {{c1 you are running b.py as main script thus package is None the relative import doesn't work:: reason?}}.
start a python terminal, and run `import b` will {{c1 not work either :: work or not}} as the {{c1 `__package__`}} variable is {{c1 empty}}, indicating that {{c1 it is not in any package,(why?)}} thus python {{c1 doesn't know what parent package is}}
run `python -m b` will {{c1 not work because agian}}, as {{c1 `__package__` is empty}}

in directory src/

run `python -m a.b` or `import a.b` will {{c3 not work}} as {{c3 the `__package__` is set to be a and relative import can not go beyond a}}

in directory project/
run `python src/a/b.py` will {{c2 not work}} because{{c2 again you are running it as main script}} 
start a python terminal, and import b with syntax {{c2 import src.a.b}} will {{c2 work}} as {{c2 `__package__` variable is set to src.a}}
run `python -m` with syntax {{c2 `python -m src.a.b`}} will {{c2 work}} because {{c2 of the same reason as `__package__` variable is set to src.a}}, this is [why we sometimes run python -m]([[663f5e26-8877-487a-b708-82bfe22981a3]])
